methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series E → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.4x) to $375M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 1 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Quantinuum
private.rationale: Same sector (Quantum Computing), same stage (Series E), post-money $10B

data.narrative:
Photonic Inc is a Series E quantum computing company led by Stephanie Simmons and Paul Terry that has raised $375M CAD to date. The quantum computing sector comprises 10 comparable entities: 3 public companies (IONQ, RGTI, QBTS) and 7 private peers including Quantinuum, IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion.

Valuation analysis employed stage-progression methodology, the only applicable triangulation approach given data constraints. This method applies a 1.4x step-up multiplier typical of Series E advancement to the $375M total capital raised as a proxy baseline, yielding an implied value of $525M. The peer-median approach was not applicable; while 7 same-sector peers exist, insufficient comparable datapoints met narrow filtering criteria (same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-tier valuation), and applying an all-sector median would introduce unacceptable noise.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $446M (low) to $604M (median $525M), anchored to stage progression alone. Key uncertainties stem from the single-method limitation: the absence of sufficient same-stage and adjacent-stage comparable transactions in quantum computing prevents cross-validation through peer benchmarking. The robustness of this range depends heavily on the appropriateness of the 1.4x multiplier for Photonic Inc's specific capital efficiency and technology maturity relative to typical Series E progression in the sector.
